segments.s517-g89: همان is a demonstrative here meaning “the same” (or “the very”), placed before a coordinated list. دوده is a noun “smoke/soot” (here likely “smoke” or the smoky dust of battle). The coordinating و links the nouns; لشکر is a noun “army/host.” کشورش is کشور + enclitic possessive -ش, so “his country/realm.” The whole phrase is a nominal, elliptical list with no verb — it picks up contextually from the surrounding lines.
segments.s517-g90: همان repeats “the same,” applying to the following pair. خسروی here is adjectival/attributive “kingly, royal” (derived from خسرو) modifying قامت, which is a noun meaning “stature” or “bearing.” و is the coordinator “and.” منظرش is منظر with the possessive clitic -ش (“his aspect/appearance/scene”), so “his aspect/appearance.” As with the previous segment, this is a nominal phrase; the clauses are parallel and elliptical (no finite verb).
segments.s517-g65: This is a negated present/subjunctive copula used as a general statement: 'نباشد' (not be/remain) is placed before the subject for poetic word order. 'جهان' is the subject ('the world' / this life), 'بر کسی' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for/with respect to anyone', and 'پایدار' is the predicate adjective 'lasting'. Together they mean 'the world does not remain lasting for anyone.'
segments.s517-g66: Poetic inversion: 'همه' quantifies what remains; 'نام نیکو' ('good name' = reputation) is the noun phrase; 'بود' is the past copula; and 'یادگار' names the remaining thing as 'a memorial' or memento. The line means roughly 'all that remains as a memorial was a good name.'
segments.s517-g67: 'کجا' is the interrogative 'where'. 'شد' here functions as a past/result verb in the idiom 'کجا شد ...' meaning 'where have ... gone/what has become of ...'. The proper names follow in a series linked by 'و' (and).
segments.s517-g68: A string of nouns in apposition or as an emphatic listing: 'مهان' (here an epithet for eminent people), 'عرب' (Arabs), and 'خسروان عجم' ('the kings/khosroes of Persia'). The line is elliptical (no new verb) and rhetorically asks where all these people are.
segments.s517-g69: 'کجا' again asks 'where'. 'آن' is the demonstrative 'those/the', 'بزرگان' is the plural noun 'great ones' or 'notables', and 'ساسانیان' specifies the dynasty (the Sasanian people). The phrase forms a single noun phrase: 'Where are those great Sasanids?'
segments.s517-g70: A prepositional range: 'ز' (poetic 'from'), 'بهرامیان' (the Bahramids), 'تا' ('until/as far as'), 'به' ('to/at'), 'سامانیان' (the Samanids). Together they indicate the span 'from the Bahramids to the Samanids.'
segments.s517-g71: 'نکوهیده‌تر' is a comparative/adjective meaning 'more reproached' / 'most censured' and precedes the noun for poetic emphasis. 'شاه ضحاک' is the noun phrase 'King Zahhak', and 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The line asserts that Zahhak was especially blamed.
segments.s517-g72: 'که' introduces a relative clause 'who/for he', explaining why he was censured. 'بیدادگر' is a descriptive noun/adjective 'tyrant/cruel one', 'بود' is the copula 'was', 'و' joins the two predicates, and 'ناپاک' is another predicate 'impure/immoral' followed again by 'بود'. The clause gives two reasons: he was tyrannical and unclean/immoral.
segments.s517-g81: ازین is از + این ('from this'); here it introduces the source or heading ('from this book/record'). نامه is used as 'book/treatise' (a written account). شاه دشمن‌گداز is a noun phrase in which شاه ('king') is qualified by the compound adjective دشمن‌گداز ('enemy‑destroying' — a poetic epithet). Word order is source + title: 'from this book: the king, enemy‑destroying'.
segments.s517-g82: که introduces a wish clause here ('that / may it be that'). بادا is an optative particle meaning 'may (it be)'. همه‌ساله is an adverbial ('every year'). بر تخت ناز is a prepositional phrase: بر + تخت ('on the throne') and ناز is an attributive noun meaning 'pride/pomp' — 'on the throne of pride/indulgence'. The clause expresses a wish for the king's yearly continuance on the throne.
segments.s517-g83: همه مردم is 'all the people/everyone'. از خانها here means 'from the khans' or 'including the khans' (the phrase emphasizes that even high persons participated). شد plus به دشت is an intransitive past/eventive use meaning 'went/turned toward the plain' (شد here indicates movement/happening). Word order: subject — (optional inclusive phrase) — verb — direction.
segments.s517-g84: نیایش 'prayer/supplication' is the subject of the verb برگذشت. همی is a frequentative/adverb meaning 'continuously/still'. ز آسمان is a poetic word order for 'from the sky/heaven' (z = از). برگذشت is a compound verb (بر + گذشت) meaning 'passed by/was conveyed' — here it gives a sense of prayers rising/being passed from the sky.
segments.s517-g85: که again introduces a wish/explanation clause. جاوید بادا is an optative formula 'may (it be) eternal' with جاوید ('eternal') before the optative بادا. سر تاجدار is a noun phrase 'the crowned head' (سر 'head' + تاجدار 'crowned/sovereign'), the phrase that the wish applies to.
segments.s517-g86: خجسته is an adjective/adverb meaning 'auspicious, blessed'. برو here is an optative/predicative form (classical/poetic usage) functioning like 'may it be' or 'may it fare' — so خجسته برو together gives 'may it be blessed/auspicious'. گردش روزگار is a genitive construction: گردش ('turn, circuit') of روزگار ('time/fortune'), i.e. 'the revolving course of time/fate' which is the instrument or accompaniment of the blessing.
segments.s517-g87: ز گیتی is a poetic 'from the world' (ز = از). مبیناد is an archaic/poetic present or potential form meaning 'appears / becomes manifest' (here negative sense: nothing in the world shows up). جز means 'except'. کام خویش is 'his own desire' — کام = 'desire/wish', خویش is expressed by the separate clitic ش later; together pointing to 'his own wish' as the only thing visible in the world.
segments.s517-g88: نوشته here is a past participle/verb meaning 'is written' (or 'has written'); بر ایوانها is a locative phrase 'upon the porticoes' (بر + ایوانها). نام خویش is 'his name' (نام = name, خویش + ش = his own, with the enclitic ش explicitly marking possession). Together: 'his name is written upon the porticoes.'
segments.s517-g57: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین as adverb 'thus/so' introducing reported speech). گفت is the past 3rd-person singular of گفتن ('said') placed before the speaker (poetic word order). نوشین روان قباد is the speaker's name + epithet: نوشین‌روان = 'sweet-souled/delightful in spirit' followed by the proper name قباد.
segments.s517-g58: که introduces the content of what was said ('that/when'). چون here means 'when'/'as'. شاه را: شاه 'the king' with را marking the definite/direct object/topic. دل 'heart'. بپیچد is present/subjunctive 3rd singular of پیچیدن with the prefix ب- (classical/poetic form) meaning 'turns/turns aside' (i.e. 'turns away'). ز داد = 'from justice' (ز = poetic 'from/because of'; داد = 'justice').
segments.s517-g59: کند is the verb 'to make/do' (here 'will make' or 'makes' in a gnomic/poetic sense). چرخ 'the wheel/sky/fate' is the agent (poetic for fortune/heaven). منشور او را = 'his register/tablet/record' (منشور used as an inscribed record or official register) with او را marking possession + object. سیاه 'black/dark' is the result adjective. Word order is typical of classical Persian poetry (verb early, then agent, then object and predicate adjective).
segments.s517-g60: ستاره (a star) is the subject for نخواند; نخواند here means 'will not read/predict' (خواندن in the sense 'to read/foretell'). ورا is the enclitic pronoun 'to him/it' (object). نیز ... شاه appended at the end indicates 'nor the king either' — the verb (نخواند) applies also to the king (classical poetics permits the second subject to follow). Thus: 'no star will read/predict him, nor will the king.'
segments.s517-g61: This nominal clause states existence with بود ('was'). ستم نامه here is a compound 'book/ledger of oppression' (ستم 'oppression' + نامه 'record/book'). عزل شاهان modifies نامه: 'the dismissal/deposition of kings' (عزل = removal/dismissal; شاهان = 'kings'). بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s517-g62: چو = 'as/when' (here 'as' / 'just as'). درد دل = 'the pain of the heart' (emotional suffering). بیگناهان = 'the innocent' (plural). بود again is the past copula 'was'. The clause parallels the previous one: 'as there was the ledger of oppression, so there was the pain in the hearts of the innocent.'
segments.s517-g63: بماناد is an optative/archaic 3rd-person form meaning 'may ... remain' or 'let ... remain' (a wish). تا جاودان = 'until/so as to be eternal' i.e. 'forever'; تا links the desire to the duration (here 'may it remain eternal'). این گهر = 'this jewel/gem' (این 'this'; گهر 'gem').
segments.s517-g64: A three-term apposition describing the 'gohar': هنرمند 'artful/skillful' (here 'of artistry'), و = 'and', بادانش is a compound (با + دانش) meaning 'with knowledge' → 'learned'/'of learning', و = 'and', دادگر 'just' (often used of a just ruler). The sequence lists qualities of the jewel—artistry, learning, and justice.
segments.s517-g25: که is the relative/connecting particle 'who/that' introducing a descriptive clause. پیروز نامست is a contracted poetic form for پیروز نام است ('is named Pīrūz'), where نامست fuses نام + است; this is an archaic/poetic compression. و پیروزبخت appends a second epithet (پیروزبخت 'fortunate') in coordination; the whole segment describes the person by name and epithet.
segments.s517-g26: همی here is a classical adverb meaning 'continually/always' (not the emphatic 'indeed'). بگذرد is the present habitual sense 'passes' (گذریدن → to pass). تیر او is the noun 'his arrow' with the pronoun او as possessor; بر درخت is a prepositional phrase marking the target ('against/on the tree'). The clause states a recurring/resulting fact rather than an explicit optative (there is no باد here).
segments.s517-g27: همیشه is fronted as an adverb 'always'. تن شاه is a possessive noun phrase 'the king's body' (تن = body). بی‑رنج is a compound adjective 'free from pain/untroubled'. باد at the end is the optative particle 'may' (a standard wish-form in Persian poetry), so the whole clause reads 'may the king's body always be free from pain.'
segments.s517-g28: نشستش is the noun/nominal 'his seat/sitting' (نشست with the enclitic -ش 'his'). همه functions as 'whole/entire' modifying نشستش. بر سر گنج is a locative/prepositional phrase 'upon the top of the treasure' (سر گنج = 'the top/head of the treasure'). The final باد is again the optative 'may', completing the wish: 'may his whole seat be upon the treasure.'
segments.s517-g29: همیدون is a proper name. سپهدار is a title 'sepahdār/commander' placed after the name. او is the third‑person pronoun 'he' (here somewhat resumptive/emphatic). شاد باد is the optative formula 'may he be happy' (شاد = happy; باد = may).
segments.s517-g30: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic -ش). روشن is an adjective 'bright/clear', so دلش روشن = 'may his heart be bright/cheerful'. و links the two wishes. گنجش is 'his treasure' (گنج + enclitic -ش) and آباد is the adjective 'prosperous/flourishing'; the final باد is the optative 'may', so the clause reads 'may his heart be bright and his treasure prosper.'
segments.s517-g31: چنین 'thus/so' opens the clause. تا introduces a temporal limit 'until/so long as'. به پایست uses پایست (here verbal/adjectival 'to stand/endure') with به marking the resultant/standing state; گردان سپهر is 'the revolving/turning sky' (گردان modifying سپهر). Together the phrase means 'so long as the revolving heavens stand/endure.'
segments.s517-g32: ازین is the fused prepositional phrase از این 'from this'. تخمه means 'seed/stock/offspring' (i.e., lineage). هرگز is the adverb 'never'. مبراد is an archaic/obscure form here functioning as a negative jussive/passive sense 'be not removed / may it not be taken away' (the exact etymology is uncertain, but context requires 'do not remove/take away'). مهر is 'affection/favour' (here 'love or favour'). The clause thus prays that love/favour never be taken from this line.
segments.s517-g1: کنون is an adverb meaning 'now/presently' placed before the verb for temporal emphasis. 'پادشاه جهان را' is the definite object phrase (پادشاه = 'king', جهان = 'the world' modifying king), with را marking it as the direct object. ستای is an archaic/poetic form of the verb 'ستودن' used here as an imperative/optative ('praise!' or 'let one praise'), so the line issues the exhortation to praise the named king.
segments.s517-g2: The clause lists three domains introduced by repeated prepositions به ('to/for/in the sphere of') and linked by و ('and'). گرای at the end is a predicative element meaning 'inclined to' or '-oriented', so the whole phrase qualifies the king as one inclined toward battle (رزم), feasting/banquet (بزم), and knowledge (دانش). The repeated به emphasizes each domain separately; the structure is typical of classical Persian epithets.
segments.s517-g3: A sequence of epithets and a proper name: سرافراز ('exalted, honored') precedes the personal name محمود (Mahmud). فرخنده‌رای is a compound epithet meaning 'fortunate/auspicious-minded' or 'of auspicious counsel' and attaches to the name as a descriptive title. Classical Persian allows stacked epithets before/after names in this way.
segments.s517-g4: کزویست is an archaic contraction of کز وی است ('which is from him' / 'whose ... is'), functioning as a relative connector introducing a statement about what pertains to him. نام here means 'name' in the sense of reputation/fame, and بزرگی is 'greatness' (qualifying the name). به جای literally 'in place' = 'established/standing in rank' — together: 'whose great name is established/held in place.'
segments.s517-g5: جهاندار ('world-ruler' / sovereign) is a titular noun, followed by the kunya/name ابوالقاسم (Abū al-Qāsim). پر خرد is an adjectival phrase: پر ('full of') precedes خرد ('wisdom'), so together 'full of wisdom' describes the sovereign. The adjective phrase uses the classical Persian construction پر + noun = 'full of [noun]'.
segments.s517-g6: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a clause about the sovereign. رایش refers to 'his counsel/judgment' (رای = opinion/decision) and is the subject of the clause. همی is a classical auxiliary/particle marking continued or habitual action ('always/indeed' here). از خرد ('from wisdom') gives the source or conformity of that judgment, and برخورد in this archaic poetic usage means something like 'meets/comes into contact with' or 'is in accordance with' — so: 'whose judgment is in accord with wisdom.'
segments.s517-g7: همی باد is a conventional optative formula in classical Persian: همی (an older continuous/emphatic particle) + باد (the optative 'may it be') expresses a wish. تا here introduces the scope/result 'that/as far as', and جاودان شاد دل is the desired state: جاودان ('eternal') modifying شاد دل ('joyful heart'). Word order places the adverbial/optative first, then the goal.
segments.s517-g8: This clause describes the resulting condition of the heart. ز (poetic variant of از) = 'from', repeated for emphasis: 'from suffering and from sorrow'. گشته is a perfect/participial form of شدن meaning 'has become' / 'become', so گشته آزاد دل = 'the heart having become free' (آزاد = 'free'). The final دل repeats/echoes 'heart' for emphasis — a common poetic device.
segments.s517-g17: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning "when" introducing a temporal clause. آیدش = آید + ـش (enclitic) literally "comes to him/it," with آید used here as a general/present-tense narrative verb. پیش جنگ آورد is a verb phrase with آورد (past/simple) at the end: پیش here functions adverbially "to the front/forward," and جنگ is the object/scene; the phrase means "he brought/bore (himself) forward into battle."
segments.s517-g18: سر شهریاران is a noun phrase (سر = "head" + شهریاران = "the chiefs/lords") and functions as the object. به چنگ آورد is an idiomatic verb cluster: به + چنگ ("into the claw/into the grasp") + آورد (past "brought/took"); together it means "seized" or "took into his grasp." Note the normal Persian SOV order with the verb آورد at the end.
segments.s517-g19: برآنکس که is a fixed construction meaning "to/for that person who" (برآنکس = "to that one" + که introducing a relative clause). بخشش کند uses بخشش ("giving/donation") with کند (present/simple auxiliary) to mean "who gives"; in Persian prose the present-tense/optative style expresses a general truth. گنج خویش = "his treasure," with خویش realized as خوی + ـش (possessive enclitic); word order is noun + possessor.
segments.s517-g20: ببخشد is a present/subjunctive or habitual third-person form of بخشیدن meaning "gives/bestows." نه‑اندیشد is negative (نه + اندیشد) meaning "he does not think/consider," where اندیشد is present/subjunctive/epic present. از رنج خویش is a prepositional phrase "of/from his suffering" with از marking source/relationship and خویش (خوی + ـش) the possessive "his."
segments.s517-g21: This is an optative clause: جهان تاجهاندار محمود باد. جهان ("the world") is in apposition with تاجهاندار ("crown-bearer/ruler"); محمود is an epithet "praised" (here as a name/epithet). باد is the optative particle "may (it be)," placed at the end to wish/bless. Word order is elevated/poetic (noun phrases followed by the optative).
segments.s517-g22: وزو is an archaic/poetic contraction (here = وز او / از او) meaning "from him"; بخشش و داد are a coordinated noun phrase "generosity/forgiveness and justice," with موجود "present/existent" as a predicate adjective and باد again the optative particle "may (they be)." The overall sense is a wish that generosity and justice flow/come from him.
segments.s517-g23: سپهدار is the subject (title, "the commander"). چون here functions as a linking particle "as/when/in the guise of" connecting the title to the name that follows (an appositive/use of as). بوالمظفر is a proper name/honorific; بود is the past-tense copula "was." The structure is a simple nominal predication in past tense.
segments.s517-g24: سرلشکر ("chief general") is the subject; the predicate is a comparative phrase از ماه برتر بود — از introduces the standard of comparison ("than/from the moon"), برتر is the comparative adjective "superior/loftier," and بود is the past copula. The order is S + comparative phrase + copula, a normal poetic construction meaning "was loftier than the moon."
segments.s517-g33: This is a piled-up, poetic noun phrase listing generations. Each noun (پدر, پسر) is linked by the preposition بر 'upon/on' used distributively to mean 'one after another' or 'after one another' (i.e., successive fathers and successive sons). There is no verb here; the predication comes in the next segment that applies to all these nouns.
segments.s517-g34: همه is a universal subject marker 'all/everyone'. تاجدارند is a present third-person plural predicate 'they are crowned' (تاج‌دار = 'wearing a crown'); the plural ending -ند marks the verb-like predicate. و links the next predicate; پیروزگر is a predicative adjective/noun 'triumphant, victorious' describing the same referents.
segments.s517-g35: گذشته is a verbal/adjectival participle meaning 'has passed' or 'is past' used impersonally; ز شوال = 'since Shawwal' (ز = 'from/since'). ده با چهار is a poetic way of giving the numeral 'ten and four' (i.e. fourteen), with با joining the numerals as 'and'.
segments.s517-g36: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one' (here 'a single'). آفرین is an exclamation/noun 'acclamation' or 'bravo'. باد is the optative particle 'may (there be)' marking a wish. بر introduces the beneficiary/target 'for' or 'upon', and شهریار is 'the king' — together forming the formulaic wish 'May there be one acclamation for the king.'
segments.s517-g37: کزین = 'from this' (introducing source/reason). مژده 'good news' is the object/source of the announcement. دادیم is first-person plural past 'we gave/announced' (دادن used here in the sense 'announce'). رسم خراج is a noun phrase 'the custom/practice of tribute' (رسم governing خراج).
segments.s517-g38: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining that announcement. فرمان is the noun 'decree/command' and بد here is an archaic past form (بُد) of بودن meaning 'was' (i.e. 'there was'); the construction is therefore 'there was a decree'. از شاه 'from the king' marks the source/agent and با فر و تاج 'with glory and crown' is an accompanying circumstantial phrase. (Note: the provided sense list for بد does not include this archaic copula sense, so that item is treated as irregular here.)
segments.s517-g39: که is the conjunction/relative 'that' linking this clause to the previous sentence. سالی is an adverbial/quantifier 'annually' or 'a year's (worth)'; it precedes خراجی ('tribute/tax') which is the object/topic of the clause. نخواهند is the negative third‑person plural (here future/optative sense) 'they will not/would not ask/exact'; بیش 'more' follows the verb phrase and gives the comparative 'more than'. Together the word order expresses 'that they will not exact more than an annual tribute.'
segments.s517-g40: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دین‌دار ('dīn-dār') is an adjective/noun 'pious, devout (one)'; بیدار literally 'awake/alert' is another epithet attached to the same group. وز here is written as a unit but functions as the archaic cluster 'و ز' ('and from'), introducing the next group. مرد کیش is a genitival phrase 'men/persons of the creed/faith' (مرد modified by کیش). The whole segment means 'from the pious and from men of the faith.'
segments.s517-g73: فریدون فرخ is a name with an epithet: the proper name فریدون followed by the adjective فرخ (“fortunate”) as a title. ستایش (object) precedes the verb ببرد; ببرد here is a poetic/perfective use of برد meaning “gain/receive” (word order object–verb is normal in Persian poetry).
segments.s517-g74: بمرد او is a compact verbal clause meaning “he died” (ب- prefixed to مرد is a poetic/peripheral form indicating the event of dying) followed by the pronoun او. و links the two clauses adversatively (“but/and”). جاوید نامش نمرد: adjective جاوید precedes the noun نام (his fame/name) and نمرد is the negated past ‘did not die’, so ‘his eternal name did not perish’.
segments.s517-g75: سخن is the subject meaning ‘poem/words’ and ماند is the past of ماندن ‘remain’. اندر جهان is a locative phrase ‘in the world’, and یادگار functions as a predicate/complement ‘as a memorial’ — overall: ‘words/poetry remain in the world as a memorial’.
segments.s517-g76: سخن is repeated as the thing being compared; بهتر از is the comparative ‘better than’, with از introducing the standard of comparison. گوهر شاهوار is a noun (گوهر, ‘gem’) followed by the adjective شاهوار (‘regal/lofty’), i.e. ‘a regal jewel’.
segments.s517-g77: ستایش نبرد here means ‘praise was not won/ he did not win praise’ (نبرد is a negative verb form in this context: ن + برد). آنک is a causal particle ‘for/that’, and بیداد بود is a simple past clause ‘there was injustice/oppression’ giving the reason. (Note: the lexical list included a different lemma for نبرد; function here is verbal negation of برد.)
segments.s517-g78: به گنج و به تخت: repeated preposition به marks the objects/means ‘with/through treasure and with a throne’; مهی is an adjective modifying تخت (‘moon‑like/beautiful throne’). شاد بود is the past tense copula with adjective: ‘was happy/joyful’.
segments.s517-g79: گسسته is a past participle ‘broken/severed’ used with شود (here as an auxiliary) to form a passive/result state ‘was broken’. در جهان is the locative ‘in the world’, and کام اوی = ‘his desire’ (اوی as a possessive). Word order participle + auxiliary + locative + possessed noun yields ‘his desire in the world was broken’.
segments.s517-g80: نخواند is the negated form of خواند (here ‘will not call/mention’ or ‘is not summoned’); به گیتی = ‘in the world/earth’ (locative), کسی with negative context means ‘no one’, and نام اوی = ‘his name’ (possessive). Together: ‘no one in the world will name/mention him.’
segments.s517-g49: This is a pair of optative wishes. سبز باد is an optative formula (adjective + باد) meaning “may (his) head be green” — باد here is an optative particle, not the noun “wind.” The two halves are joined by و “and,” and تنش بی‌گزند is a parallel nominal phrase: تن + possessive suffix + adjective “unharmed.” Word order is noun+possessive+predicate.
segments.s517-g50: منش is the noun “character” (subject/thing wished for). برگذشته here functions as a participial/adjectival phrase meaning “having surpassed / exalted above.” ز introduces the standard/limit (poetic use of ز = from/than), and چرخ بلند is a poetic image “the lofty wheel (of fate/heaven).” The whole phrase qualifies منش: “character that has risen above the lofty wheel.”
segments.s517-g51: ندارد with کسی forms a negative existential sense: “there is no one who…” or “no one has/does.” خوار is a predicate adjective meaning “despising / holding in contempt,” and فال مرا (مرا+را split in the tokenization) is the object “my omen.” The literal order is negated verb + subject + predicate + object marker construction.
segments.s517-g52: This is a rhetorical question. کجا functions as “where / how” in the sense “who would / where would one…?” بشمرد is a present/subjunctive sense of ‘to count or reckon’ (used in the rhetorical question), ماه و سال مرا is the object phrase “my months and years” (مرا + را split: م is part of مرا), i.e. one’s lifetime; verb precedes object in the clause.
segments.s517-g53: نگه کن is an imperative phrase meaning “look / take heed.” که introduces the subordinate clause. این نامه تا جاودان: تا + جاودان expresses duration ‘until/for eternity’ — i.e. ‘this letter will last forever.’ Word order: imperative + complementizer + demonstrative + noun + temporal phrase.
segments.s517-g54: درفشی بود is a nominal clause in the past tense: درفشی (banner/standard) + بود “was,” meaning “served as a banner.” بر سر introduces the locus “upon the head(s) of …” and بخردان is the plural dative/genitive “the wise” — so the whole phrase means the letter “was a banner upon the heads of the wise.”
segments.s517-g55: بماند is an optative/subjunctive form meaning “may it remain / let it remain.” بسی روزگاران = “many / many ages” (بسی = much/many), and چنین “thus/so” points back to the foregoing (the letter/banner). Together: “May this remain for many ages.”
segments.s517-g56: که introduces a result/purpose clause: خوانند is present/subjunctive plural ‘they read / recite’ (used impersonally here: ‘whoever reads’). هرکس is the indefinite subject “whoever,” and برو آفرین is an idiomatic exclamation meaning roughly “(may they) cry ‘bravo’” — برو intensifies the exclamation rather than a literal imperative “go.”
segments.s517-g9: This is a chain of titles and domains in apposition: شهنشاه (title) is followed by the names of the territories it rules. In classical/poetic Persian the linking ezafe is often omitted, so this reads as “the king/emperor of Iran and Zabulistan.” Word order is Title + governed places (no copula).
segments.s517-g10: A prepositional range: ز (poetic از) marks the start point and تا introduces the end point. مرز کابلستان is a noun phrase ‘the border/frontier of Kabulistan.’ The whole segment gives the geographical span under his rule: from X تا (to) Y.
segments.s517-g11: برو آفرین باد is an optative/exhortative formula: برو (let go forth) + آفرین (praise/acclamation) with باد as the optative particle meaning “may/let it be.” The following و بر لشکرش attaches with بر ‘upon’ to mark the recipient of that praise (his army).
segments.s517-g12: چه introduces an exclamatory/enumerative clause (‘whether/what’) and the preposition بر is repeated for each item (an emphatic list): بر خویش (on/for his own/kin), بر دوده (the middle word is archaic/poetic here; context shows it names another sphere receiving praise), و کشورش (and his country). The suffix ش is the enclitic possessive ‘his’ attached to the nouns.
segments.s517-g13: A string of epithets forming a nominative phrase: جهاندار سالار (world‑ruler, commander) followed by the pronoun او (‘he’) and then the name میر نصر. Classical style allows this appositive piling of titles before the proper name without an explicit copula.
segments.s517-g14: کزو is a relative/adverbial pronoun ‘from whom’ referring back to the named man; شادمانست is an archaic contraction (شادمان است) meaning ‘is joyful’; گردنده عصر is an agentive phrase literally ‘the turner/one who turns the age’ (گردنده as agentive adjective/noun modifying عصر). Together: ‘from whom the turning of the age is joyful.’
segments.s517-g15: دریغش نیاید is an idiomatic negative clause: literally ‘no reluctance comes to him.’ The preposition ز + infinitive بخشیدن indicates the source/reason (‘from giving’), and ایچ (an old/poetic form of هیچ) means ‘anything/at all.’ The sense: he feels no reluctance to give.
segments.s517-g16: نه آرام گیرد is a negative present construction: نه (not) + آرام گیرد (‘takes/gets rest’ — گیرد here = ‘takes’). به‌روز is an adverbial ‘by day’ / ‘in the daytime,’ and بسیچ is an intensifier ‘many/much’ (poetic), giving the sense ‘he does not rest by day at all / not even for many days.’
segments.s517-g41: بدین عهد is a prepositional phrase ('به + این' contracted) placed up front: 'by/at this age/pledge'. نوشین‌روان is the noun phrase (literally 'sweet‑souled') and تازه شد is a past-tense/perfect-like predicate ('tazeh shod' = 'became fresh/was renewed'), where شد functions as a past/preterite of شدن ('become'). Word order is prepositional phrase → subject → predicate, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s517-g42: همه کار is the noun phrase 'all affairs'. بر دیگر اندازه شد is predicative: بر (on/upon) + دیگر (other/new) + اندازه (measure/order) + شد ('became/was made'), meaning 'were put to a different measure' or 'were ordered differently'; شد again is the perfective/preterite of شدن marking the change of state.
segments.s517-g43: چو آمد is a protasis meaning 'when it came' (چو = 'when'), followed by بران ('upon/that') pointing to the mentioned time; روزگاری دراز is a postpositive nominal phrase 'a long period/time' (دراز modifying روزگاری). The clause order (چو + past verb + time phrase) is typical of narrative past in classical verse.
segments.s517-g44: همی is an adverb marking repeated or continuing action ('again/still'); بفگند is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'they cast/spread' (here 'spread out'); چادر is the object 'tent'; داد باز is a light-verb construction where داد ('gave') combines with باز ('open') to mean 'opened (wide)' or 'unfurled' the tent. The verb cluster expresses a repeated, performed action in narrative past.
segments.s517-g45: ببینی is a second‑person/formulaic narrative form meaning 'you would see' or 'one would see/think' (used rhetorically). بدین is 'from/by this' (به + این) and takes داد و نیکی ('justice and goodness') as its complement — 'from this justice and goodness'. گمان at the end ('guman') indicates 'one's supposition'—together: 'you would suppose/think'. The clause is a parenthetical inference marker.
segments.s517-g46: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); او is the third‑person subject 'he'; خلعتی is the object 'a robe (bestowed gift)'; یابد is the present/subjunctive/potential form of یافتن used here as 'may obtain/would receive' (expressing expectation); از آسمان is the source phrase 'from heaven'. The verb is in a potentialive sense rather than a plain factual present.
segments.s517-g47: که 'that' introduces a wish/continuation clause. هرگز نگردد is a negative jussive/optative sense 'may never turn/come' (نگردد here = 'befall/turn upon'); کهن stands for 'old age/ancient time' as the thing that should not come; بر + (the pronoun) indicates 'upon him' — the preposition and pronominal element are merged in the clause to express 'come upon him.' The whole is a conventional formulaic wish: 'may old age never come upon him.'
segments.s517-g48: بماند is an optative/jussive present meaning 'may ... remain' or 'let ... remain'; کلاه کیان is a genitive 'the hat/crown of the Kians' (i.e. the royal crown); بر سرش is the locative phrase 'upon his head' with ش the enclitic 'his'. The clause expresses a blessing/wish: 'may the Kayan crown remain upon his head.'

sentences.s517-s45: The same smoke, the same army and his realm; the same kingly stature and his aspect.
sentences.s517-s33: The world endures for no one; only a good name remains as a memorial.
sentences.s517-s34: Where have Fereydun, Zahhak, and Jam gone? — the great ones, the Arabs, the kings of Persia.
sentences.s517-s35: Where are those great Sasanids—from the Bahramids up to the Samanids?
sentences.s517-s36: Most reproached was King Zahhak, who was a tyrant and impure.
sentences.s517-s41: From this book, (about) the king who destroys his enemies — may he every year sit upon the throne of pride.
sentences.s517-s42: All the people, even the khans, went out to the plain; prayers continually passed up from the sky.
sentences.s517-s43: May the crowned head be eternal — blessed be it by the revolving fortunes of time.
sentences.s517-s44: From the world nothing becomes apparent except his own desire; upon the porticoes his name is written.
sentences.s517-s29: Thus said Qobad, the sweet-souled: when a king's heart turns away from justice,
sentences.s517-s30: the wheel will blacken his register; no star will read him, nor will the king either.
sentences.s517-s31: There was a ledger of oppression and the deposition of kings, as there was the pain in the hearts of the innocent.
sentences.s517-s32: May this jewel — of artistry, learning, and justice — remain forever.
sentences.s517-s13: whose name is Pīrūz and who is fortunate — his arrow always passes against the tree.
sentences.s517-s14: May the king's body always be free of pain; may his entire seat be upon the treasure.
sentences.s517-s15: May Hamīdūn, the commander, be glad; may his heart be bright and his treasure flourish.
sentences.s517-s16: So long as the revolving sky endures, may love never be removed from this stock.
sentences.s517-s1: Now, praise the king of the world — one inclined to battle, to feasting, and to knowledge.
sentences.s517-s2: The lofty Mahmoud, the auspicious-minded — whose great name is established (from him) in rank.
sentences.s517-s3: The sovereign Abū al-Qāsim, full of wisdom, whose judgment is formed from (i.e., conforms to) wisdom.
sentences.s517-s4: May it be so always — may the heart be forever joyful, the heart made free from suffering and from sorrow.
sentences.s517-s9: When battle came upon him he pushed forward into the fight and seized the chiefs' heads in his grasp.
sentences.s517-s10: To the man who shows generosity he gives his treasure; he does not consider his own suffering.
sentences.s517-s11: May Mahmoud, the world's crown-bearer, be praised; and may generosity and justice be present from him.
sentences.s517-s12: The sepahdar was Bu al-Muzaffar; the commander-in-chief was loftier than the moon.
sentences.s517-s17: Father upon father, son upon son — all are crowned and victorious.
sentences.s517-s18: Fourteen days have passed since Shawwal; may there be a single acclamation for the king.
sentences.s517-s19: From this good tidings we announced the practice of tribute; there was a decree from the king with glory and crown.
sentences.s517-s20: that they would not exact more than an annual tribute from the pious and from men of the faith.
sentences.s517-s37: Fereydun the Fortunate won praise; he died, but his eternal name did not perish.
sentences.s517-s38: A poem remains in the world as a memorial; words are better than a regal jewel.
sentences.s517-s39: He won no praise, for there was oppression; he rejoiced in treasure and on a moon‑like throne.
sentences.s517-s40: His desire in the world was broken; no one in the earth will call his name.
sentences.s517-s25: May his head be green and his body unharmed; may his character soar above the high wheel of fate.
sentences.s517-s26: Let no one scorn my omen; who could reckon my months and years?
sentences.s517-s27: Note that this letter shall remain eternal, a banner upon the heads of the wise.
sentences.s517-s28: May such a thing remain for many ages, so that whoever reads it cries ‘bravo.’
sentences.s517-s5: The Shahanshah of Iran and Zabulistan, from Kannauj to the frontier of Kabulistan.
sentences.s517-s6: May praise go forth — upon him and upon his army; whether upon himself, upon his kin/house, or upon his country.
sentences.s517-s7: The world-ruler, the commander — he is Mir Nasr — from whom the turning of the age is joyful.
sentences.s517-s8: No reluctance comes to him in giving anything; he does not take rest by day at all.
sentences.s517-s21: By this covenant a fresh, pleasant life was renewed; all affairs were set in a different order.
sentences.s517-s22: When that long period came, they once more spread/opened the tent.
sentences.s517-s23: One might conclude from this justice and goodness that he would receive a robe from heaven.
sentences.s517-s24: That old age may never come upon him; may the Kayan crown remain on his head.
